sources.label: Alert Pattern | APG | WAI | W3C
borrowed: Keep urgent recovery alerts visible without stealing focus.
Do not auto-dismiss important recovery messages.
rejected: Toast-like recovery alerts that disappear automatically.

sources.label: Alert and Message Dialogs Pattern | APG | WAI | W3C
borrowed: Reserve interruptive dialog patterns for high-stakes confirmations, not the baseline recovery workbench.
rejected: Moving recovery control into a blocking confirmation dialog.

sources.label: Dialog (Modal) Pattern | APG | WAI | W3C
borrowed: Keep focus trapped only when a real modal is necessary.
Return focus to the invoking control when a transient overlay closes.

sources.label: Accessible Rich Internet Applications (WAI-ARIA) 1.2
borrowed: Use polite `status` semantics for non-urgent recovery updates.
Keep alert semantics only for urgent or escalated recovery states.
rejected: Treating every recovery banner as assertive.

sources.label: Accessibility testing | Playwright
borrowed: Pair automated checks with explicit landmark, keyboard, and disclosure assertions.
rejected: Using a single accessibility snapshot as the whole proof set.

sources.label: Visual comparisons | Playwright
borrowed: Keep recovery variants deterministic enough for screenshot proof.
Capture separate urgent, routine, and escalated visual states.
rejected: Bundling all recovery states into one screenshot.

sources.label: Trace viewer | Playwright
borrowed: Keep route assertions explicit so traces remain useful when recovery navigation regresses.
rejected: Screenshot-only debugging for same-shell recovery flows.

sources.label: Interruption page – NHS digital service manual
borrowed: Use visually distinct interruption styling sparingly for unusual or high-stakes recovery actions.
Keep the action and back path grouped and explicit.
rejected: Treating routine reopen review as a full interruption page.

sources.label: Error summary – NHS digital service manual
borrowed: Keep blockers concise and grouped near the dominant action.
Surface the problem state near the top of the route.
rejected: Burying recovery blockers deep in secondary rail copy.

sources.label: How we write – NHS digital service manual
borrowed: Use short, direct, factual copy for recovery and patient-message previews.
Avoid metaphors or soft reassurance while the case is unresolved.
rejected: Marketing-style or atmospheric language in the recovery board.

sources.label: How we redesigned the Linear UI
borrowed: Use density, alignment, and restraint instead of extra chrome.
Let a few strong surfaces carry the work instead of many weak cards.
rejected: Stacked generic admin cards that dilute the dominant recovery signal.
